topics: Activities: To measure the resistance and impedance of an inductor with or without iron core.
Activities: To measure resistance, voltage (AC/DC), current (AC) and check continuity of a given circuit using multimeter.
Activities: To assemble a household circuit comprising three bulbs, three (on/off) switches, a fuse and a power source.
Activities: To assemble the components of a given electrical circuit.
Activities: To study the variation in potential drop with length of a wire for a steady current.

Activities: To draw the diagram of a given open circuit comprising at least a battery, resistor/rheostat, key, ammeter and voltmeter. Mark the components that are not connected in proper order and correct the circuit and also the circuit diagram.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the value of v for different values of u in case of a concave mirror and to find the focal length.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex mirror, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a convex lens by plotting graphs between u and v or between 1/u and 1/v .
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the focal length of a concave lens, using a convex lens.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine angle of minimum deviation for a given prism by plotting a graph between angle of incidence and angle of deviation.
Experiments SECTION–B: To determine refractive index of a glass slab using a travelling microscope.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using convex lens and plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To find the refractive index of a liquid using a concave mirror and a plane mirror.
Experiments SECTION–B: To draw the I-V characteristic curve for a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.
Activities SECTION–B: To identify a diode, an LED, a resistor and a capacitor from a mixed collection of such items.
Activities SECTION–B: Use of multimeter to see the unidirectional flow of current in case of a diode and an LED and check whether a given electronic component (e.g., diode) is in working order.
Activities SECTION–B: To study effect of intensity of light (by varying distance of the source) on an LDR.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe refraction and lateral deviation of a beam of light incident obliquely on a glass slab.
Activities SECTION–B: To observe diffraction of light due to a thin slit.

Activities SECTION–B: To study the nature and size of the image formed by a (i) convex lens, or (ii) concave mirror, on a screen by using a candle and a screen (for different distances of the candle from the lens/mirror).
Activities SECTION–B: To obtain a lens combination with the specified focal length by using two lenses from the given set of lenses.
subtopics: Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
Suggested Investigatory Projects: To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

topics: Record of at least 8 Experiments [with 4 from each section], to be performed by the students.
Record of at least 6 Activities [with 3 each from section A and section B], to be performed by the students.
The Report of the project carried out by the students.
Evaluation Scheme: Max. Marks: 30 Time 3 hours
Two experiments one from each section 7+7 Marks
Practical record [experiments and activities] 5 Marks
One activity from any section 3 Marks
Investigatory Project 3 Marks
Viva on experiments, activities and project 5 Marks
Total 30 marks
subtopics: Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistivity of two / three wires by plotting a graph for potential difference versus current.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find resistance of a given wire / standard resistor using metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (series) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To verify the laws of combination (parallel) of resistances using a metre bridge.
Experiments SECTION–A: To determine resistance of a galvanometer by half-deflection method and to find its figure of merit.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into a voltmeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To convert the given galvanometer (of known resistance and figure of merit) into an ammeter of desired range and to verify the same.
Experiments SECTION–A: To find the frequency of AC mains with a sonometer.

sourceSessionPlan.title: Chapter–1: Electric Charges and Fields

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces the fundamental concepts of electric charge, its properties, and the nature of electric fields. Students will explore how charges interact through forces and visualize these interactions using field lines.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Basic understanding of atomic structure (protons, electrons, neutrons)
learningOutcomes: Define electric charge and state its two types (positive and negative).
Explain the fundamental properties of charge: quantization, conservation, and additivity.
Describe the concept of an electric field and draw field lines for a single point charge.
Distinguish between conductors and insulators based on charge mobility.

teachingPlan.teachingStrategy: Demonstration with a balloon and hair to spark curiosity about invisible forces.
Direct instruction with real-life analogies (coins, magnets) and board derivations.
Visual modeling using chalk dust or simulation to show field lines.

lessonPurpose: To establish the foundation for understanding electrostatics.
To move students from macroscopic observations to microscopic particle interactions.
To introduce the concept of a 'field' as a physical entity.
teachingSequence: Hook: Rub a balloon on hair and ask why it sticks to the wall.
Define Charge: Introduce positive (protons) and negative (electrons).
Properties: Discuss Quantization (q=ne), Conservation, and Additivity.
Field Concept: Explain 'action at a distance' vs. 'field theory'.
Visuals: Draw field lines for +q and -q charges.
Differentiation: Simplify math for slow learners; introduce Coulomb's law preview for advanced learners.
guidedPractice: Ask students to identify the charge type in a rubbed glass rod vs. silk.
Have students sketch field lines for two positive charges repelling each other.
Oral check: 'If I remove an electron, does the object become positive or negative?'

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction to Charge
teacherPrompt: Why does a balloon stick to your hair after rubbing it?
What invisible force is at work here?
explanation: Matter contains charged particles: protons (+) and electrons (-).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Charge is a fundamental property of matter.
examples: Rubbing a plastic comb on dry hair attracts paper bits.
Lightning is a massive discharge of static electricity.
boardWork: Definition: Charge (q) is the property causing electrostatic force.
boardSteps: Write 'Charge: Property of matter' on board.
Draw a simple atom with + and - signs.
solutionFlow: Identify the source of charge (excess/deficit of electrons).
State the sign based on electron movement.
proofSteps: Observation: Rubbed objects attract light objects.
Inference: A new property (charge) has been created.
checkUnderstanding: If an atom loses an electron, what is its net charge?
activity: Quick pair-share: Rub a pen on your sleeve and try to pick up paper scraps.

conceptFlow.definition: Charge exists in discrete packets, not continuous amounts.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Total charge (q) is always an integer multiple of the elementary charge (e). Formula: q = ne, where n is an integer and e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C.
conceptFlow.importance: Explains why we cannot have half an electron's worth of charge in isolation.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: Fundamental to understanding atomic interactions and modern electronics.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Builds on the knowledge that atoms have electrons.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Leads to current (I = dq/dt) in later chapters.
teacherMoves: Emphasize that n cannot be 1.5 or 2.3.
examples: A body with -3e charge has 3 extra electrons.
A body with +2e charge is missing 2 electrons.
visuals: Stack of coins analogy: You can have 1 coin or 2, but not 1.5 coins.
proofSteps: Millikan's Oil Drop Experiment proved this.
No experiment has ever found a charge less than e.

conceptFlow.definition: The region around a charge where its influence can be felt by other charges.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: A charge creates an electric field (E) in the surrounding space. Another charge placed in this field experiences a force (F = qE).
conceptFlow.importance: Replaces 'action at a distance' with a local interaction model.
observedIn: Dust particles moving near charged rods
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: Essential for understanding forces without physical contact.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Extends the concept of force to a field concept.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Directly leads to Gauss's Law and potential energy.
teacherMoves: Draw arrows radiating from a positive charge.
examples: Sun's gravity field affects Earth without touching it.
visuals: Diagram of radial lines coming out of +q and going into -q.
solutionFlow: Draw lines starting perpendicular to the surface.
proofSteps: Field lines never cross (unique direction at a point).
Density of lines indicates field strength.

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines
geometryDiagrams.caption: Visualizing the electric field around a point charge

classroomQuestions.question: Can a body have a charge of 1.5e?
answerPoints: Charge must be an integer multiple of e.

classroomQuestions.question: In which direction do field lines point for a negative charge?

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Field lines are physical wires or strings.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Clarify that field lines are imaginary constructs used to visualize the direction and strength of the field.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Like charges attract if they are close enough.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Reiterate Coulomb's Law: Like charges ALWAYS repel, regardless of distance (in electrostatics).

slowLearners: Use the 'coin stack' analogy for quantization.
Focus on drawing simple radial lines without complex math.
averageLearners: Draw field lines for single charges accurately.
advancedLearners: Discuss why field lines cannot intersect.
Predict the behavior of a dipole in an external field.

teacherTips: Use a Van de Graaff generator if available for a dramatic demo.
Emphasize that 'charge' is a property, not a substance.
misconceptions: Confusing electric field with magnetic field.
Thinking neutral objects have no charge at all (they do, just balanced).
formativeChecks: Exit ticket: Draw one field line.
assessmentQuestions: State the principle of conservation of charge with an example.
blackboardSummary: 3. Electric field lines go Out from +, In to -.

timePlan.purpose: Refresh atomic structure and introduce static electricity.
Deep dive into Quantization and Field Lines.

sessionSummary: We learned that charge is discrete, conserved, and creates an invisible field around it.

endOfClassRecap.prompt: Electric field lines originate from ______ charges and terminate at ______ charges.

nextSessionBridge: Next session: We will calculate the force between two point charges using Coulomb's Law.

learningOutcomes: Students will define electric charge and distinguish between positive and negative types.
Students will explain the quantization of charge (q=ne) and conservation of charge.
Students will describe the concept of an electric field and draw field lines for point charges.
Students will differentiate between conductors and insulators based on charge mobility.
sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine holding a balloon. You rub it against your hair, and suddenly, it sticks to the wall as if glued by magic! What is this invisible force? Today, we uncover the secret of 'Electric Charges'—the fundamental property that makes lightning strike, phones work, and atoms hold together. We will move from seeing static electricity in our daily lives to understanding the invisible fields that govern them.

theory.overview: Electric charge is a fundamental property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Charges interact via attraction (opposites) and repulsion (likes).
keyPoints: Charge is quantized: q = ne, where n is an integer.
Total charge in an isolated system is conserved.
Electric field lines represent the direction of force on a positive test charge.
theory.detailedContent: The concept of electric charge begins with the atom. Protons carry a positive charge (+e), and electrons carry a negative charge (-e). When an object gains electrons, it becomes negatively charged; when it loses electrons, it becomes positively charged. A crucial property is quantization: you cannot have half an electron's charge. The total charge Q on any body is always an integral multiple of the elementary charge e (1.6 x 10^-19 C). Another key principle is conservation: in an isolated system, the net charge remains constant; it can only be transferred, not created or destroyed. Finally, we introduce the Electric Field. A charge creates a region around it called the electric field. If another charge enters this region, it feels a force. We visualize this using field lines: they start from positive charges and end at negative charges. The density of these lines indicates the strength of the field.

activities.name: The Balloon Stick Challenge
instructions: Students rub a plastic balloon on their hair or wool sweater for 10 seconds.
They attempt to stick the balloon to a wall or ceiling.
Class discussion: Why did it stick? What happened to the electrons?

activities.name: Field Line Sketching
instructions: Draw a positive charge (+) on the board.
Students draw 4-6 field lines radiating outward, ensuring they are perpendicular to the charge surface.
Repeat for a negative charge (-), drawing lines converging inward.
Compare: Note that lines never cross.

studentLessonNotes.title: Electric Charges and Fields: The Invisible Force
studentLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session explores the fundamental nature of electric charge, how charges interact through Coulomb's Law, and the concept of electric fields that mediate these forces.
studentLessonNotes.introduction: Welcome to Chapter 1! Imagine holding two magnets; you can feel them push or pull without touching. Electricity works similarly but with tiny particles called electrons and protons. In this session, we will uncover the secrets of 'charge,' learn how to calculate the force between charges using Coulomb's Law, and visualize the invisible 'electric fields' that surround every charged object.
learningObjectives: Understand the fundamental properties of electric charge (quantization and conservation).
Apply Coulomb's Law to calculate electrostatic force between point charges.
Define and visualize the concept of an Electric Field.
quickRecall: Matter is made of atoms containing protons (+) and electrons (-).
Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Force is a vector quantity (has magnitude and direction).
easyToRemember: Charge is quantized: Q = n × e (where e is the charge of one electron).
Electric Field lines start from positive and end at negative.

comparisonTables.title: Conductors vs. Insulators
rows: Free to move throughout the material
Cannot move freely; bound to atoms

formulaCards.title: Coulomb's Law
formulaCards.meaning: The force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
formulaCards.whenToUse: When calculating the electrostatic force between two stationary point charges in a vacuum or air.

formulaCards.title: Electric Field Intensity
formulaCards.meaning: The electric field at a point is the force experienced per unit positive test charge placed at that point.
formulaCards.whenToUse: When determining the strength and direction of an electric field created by a source charge.

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines: Positive Charge
geometryDiagrams.caption: Radial lines emerging from a positive point charge (+Q)

geometryDiagrams.title: Electric Field Lines: Dipole
geometryDiagrams.caption: Curved lines connecting a positive charge (+) to a negative charge (-)

proofSteps: Coulomb's Law is an experimental law derived from observations of charged spheres.
The force acts along the line joining the two charges (central force).
Newton's Third Law applies: Force on q1 by q2 is equal and opposite to force on q2 by q1.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring vector direction in Coulomb's Law.
commonMistakes.correction: Always determine if the force is attractive (opposite charges) or repulsive (like charges) before calculating magnitude.
commonMistakes.example: Two positive charges repel; the force vectors point away from each other.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing Electric Field (E) with Electric Force (F).
commonMistakes.correction: Force depends on the test charge (F = qE); Field is a property of space independent of the test charge.
commonMistakes.example: If you double the test charge, Force doubles, but Field remains constant.

sections.heading: Electric Charge: The Source of Electricity
sections.explanation: Electric charge is an intrinsic property of matter that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. There are two types: Positive (protons) and Negative (electrons).
keyPoints: Like charges repel; opposite charges attract.
Charge is quantized: Q = n × e, where e = 1.6 × 10^-19 C.
Total charge in an isolated system is conserved.
examples: Rubbing a glass rod with silk gives it a positive charge (loses electrons).
Rubbing a plastic rod with fur gives it a negative charge (gains electrons).
sections.whyItMatters: Without charge, there would be no electricity, no atoms holding together, and no light.
sections.detailedExplanation: You cannot have half an electron's charge. Any charge Q must be an integer multiple of the elementary charge e. If you rub a balloon on your hair, electrons move from hair to balloon. The balloon becomes negative, and the hair becomes positive. The total charge before and after remains zero.
observedIn: Static electricity shocks when touching a doorknob.
Hair standing up after removing a wool hat.
visualSupport: Diagram showing electrons moving from one object to another during friction.

visualAssets.prompt: A simple illustration showing two spheres. Sphere A has extra red dots (electrons) and is labeled negative. Sphere B has fewer red dots and is labeled positive. An arrow shows electrons moving from B to A.
visualAssets.alt: Illustration of charge transfer by friction

importantNotes: Charge is a scalar quantity (no direction, only magnitude).
memoryTechniques: Remember 'P' for Positive (Protons) and 'N' for Negative (Electrons).
conceptSummary: Charge is quantized, conserved, and comes in two types.

sections.heading: Coulomb's Law: The Force Between Charges
sections.explanation: Coulomb's Law describes the electrostatic force between two stationary point charges. It states that the force is directly proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
keyPoints: Force acts along the line joining the centers.
examples: If you double the distance between two charges, the force becomes 1/4th of the original.
If you double both charges, the force becomes 4 times stronger.
sections.whyItMatters: This law is the foundation for understanding how atoms hold together and how electrical circuits work.
sections.detailedExplanation: The constant k (or 1/4πε₀) depends on the medium. In air or vacuum, k ≈ 9 × 10⁹ N·m²/C². The force is a vector, meaning it has direction. If charges are opposite, the force pulls them together; if same, it pushes them apart.
observedIn: The attraction between a charged comb and small pieces of paper.
Repulsion between two similarly charged balloons.
visualSupport: Diagram showing two charges with arrows indicating force direction (attractive vs. repulsive).

visualAssets.prompt: Two circles labeled +q and -q. Arrows point from each charge towards the other, labeled 'Attractive Force'. Another diagram shows +q and +q with arrows pointing away from each other, labeled 'Repulsive Force'.
visualAssets.alt: Visual representation of attractive and repulsive forces

importantNotes: Valid only for point charges or spherically symmetric charge distributions.
Medium matters: Force is weaker in water than in air.
memoryTechniques: Think 'Inverse Square': Double distance -> Quarter force.
conceptSummary: Force increases with charge, decreases rapidly with distance.

sections.heading: Electric Field: The Invisible Influence
sections.explanation: An electric field is a region around a charged object where another charge experiences a force. It is a vector quantity defined as Force per unit positive charge.
keyPoints: Direction of E is the direction of force on a positive test charge.
examples: The field around a lightning bolt extends for miles.
The field inside a charged metal sphere is zero.
sections.whyItMatters: Fields allow us to understand action-at-a-distance without needing physical contact between objects.
sections.detailedExplanation: Imagine the charge creates a 'field' in space. If you place a positive test charge in this field, it feels a push or pull. The strength of the field is how strong that push/pull is per unit charge. Field lines are imaginary lines drawn to visualize the field; they start at positive charges and end at negative charges.
observedIn: The way dust particles align near a charged TV screen.
The deflection of an electron beam in a CRT monitor.
visualSupport: Diagram showing radial lines coming out from a positive charge and going into a negative charge.

visualAssets.prompt: A central positive charge with arrows radiating outward. A second diagram shows a dipole with curved lines connecting the positive to the negative charge.

importantNotes: Field lines are closer together where the field is stronger.
No two field lines can intersect.
memoryTechniques: Positive charges are 'sources' (lines start); Negative charges are 'sinks' (lines end).
conceptSummary: Electric field maps the force a charge would feel at any point in space.

definitions.definition: The principle that electric charge comes in discrete packets (multiples of the elementary charge e) and cannot be continuous.
Imaginary curves drawn such that the tangent at any point gives the direction of the electric field vector at that point.

workedExamples.title: Calculating Force Between Two Charges
workedExamples.problem: Two point charges, q1 = +2 μC and q2 = -3 μC, are placed 0.5 m apart in air. Calculate the magnitude of the force between them.
steps: Identify the values and convert units to SI (Coulombs).
Substitute values into Coulomb's Law formula.
reasoning: We use the absolute value of charges because we are calculating magnitude.
Since signs are opposite, the force is attractive.
workedExamples.explanation: The negative sign indicates attraction, but for magnitude, we ignore it. The force pulls the two charges together with a strength of 0.216 Newtons.

conceptMap: Charge -> Coulomb's Law -> Force -> Electric Field
quickRecap: Force drops by 4x if distance doubles.

selfCheckQuestions: If the distance between two charges is halved, how does the force change?
Why can't electric field lines cross each other?
What happens to the total charge of a system if you add +5C and -3C?
quickSummary: Electric charge is quantized and conserved.
Coulomb's Law governs the force between charges.
Electric fields visualize the influence of charges on space.

fillInTheBlanks.prompt: The SI unit of electric charge is the ________.
Electric field lines originate from ________ charges and terminate at ________ charges.

mcqQuestions.question: If the distance between two point charges is doubled, the force becomes:
Which of the following is true about electric field lines?
options: A. They can cross each other.
B. They start from negative and end at positive.
C. They are closer where the field is stronger.
mcqQuestions.answer: C. They are closer where the field is stronger.

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: State the principle of conservation of charge.
veryShortAnswerQuestions.answer: The total electric charge in an isolated system remains constant over time.

veryShortAnswerQuestions.question: What is the value of k in Coulomb's law?

didYouKnow: The charge of a single electron is so small that you need about 6.25 billion billion electrons to make just 1 Coulomb of charge!
summary: Mastered the basics of charge, Coulomb's Law, and Electric Fields.

homework.title: Coulomb's Law Application
learningOutcomeIds: Apply Coulomb's law to calculate force between point charges
homework.instructions: Two point charges q1 = +2 μC and q2 = -4 μC are placed 0.5 meters apart in air. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the electrostatic force acting on each charge.
homework.question: Calculate the electrostatic force between two point charges of +2 μC and -4 μC separated by a distance of 0.5 m in air.
homework.answerSpace: Show all steps including formula substitution and unit conversion.
homework.visualRequirement: Draw a diagram showing the two charges, their positions, and the direction of the force vectors acting on them.

homework.title: Properties of Electric Charge
learningOutcomeIds: Explain the fundamental properties of electric charge
homework.instructions: Answer the following questions based on the properties of electric charge.
homework.question: a) State the principle of conservation of charge. b) Can a body have a charge of 0.5e? Justify your answer.
homework.answerSpace: Write concise answers with relevant examples.
homework.expectedResponse: a) Total charge in an isolated system remains constant. b) No, because charge is quantized (q = ne where n is an integer).

homework.title: Electric Field Lines
learningOutcomeIds: Draw and interpret electric field lines
homework.instructions: Draw the electric field lines for a dipole (two equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance). Label the regions of strong and weak fields.
homework.question: Sketch the pattern of electric field lines for an electric dipole. Mark the direction of the field at any point on the axial line.
homework.answerSpace: Provide a clear, labelled diagram with arrows indicating direction.
homework.visualRequirement: Draw a diagram showing two charges (+q and -q) and the curved field lines emerging from positive and entering negative charge. Include at least 3 field lines on each side.
homework.expectedResponse: Diagram showing curved lines from + to -, denser near charges, straight lines on axis.

homework.title: Electric Flux and Gauss's Law
learningOutcomeIds: Understand the concept of electric flux
homework.instructions: Read the assertion and reason carefully. Select the correct option.
homework.question: Assertion (A): The net electric flux through a closed surface enclosing no charge is zero. Reason (R): Electric field lines enter and leave a closed surface in equal numbers if no charge is enclosed inside.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
homework.answerSpace: Select the correct option and write a one-line justification.
homework.expectedResponse: Option 1. Both are true, and R correctly explains why flux is zero.

homework.title: Static Electricity in Daily Life
learningOutcomeIds: Connect theoretical concepts to real-world phenomena
homework.instructions: Explain the phenomenon of static electricity using the concept of charge transfer. Provide one real-life example.
homework.question: Why does a comb rubbed with dry hair attract small pieces of paper? Explain this using the concept of electric charges.
homework.answerSpace: Write a short paragraph explaining the charge separation and attraction mechanism.
homework.expectedResponse: Rubbing transfers electrons from hair to comb, making comb negatively charged. This induces opposite charge on paper pieces causing attraction.

homework.title: Observing Electrostatic Attraction
homework.instructions: Perform a simple activity at home to observe electrostatic attraction.
homework.question: Rub a plastic ruler with wool or dry hair and bring it near small pieces of paper. Observe the effect. Record your observation in one sentence.
homework.visualRequirement: Draw a simple sketch of the setup (ruler, hair/wool, paper pieces).
homework.expectedResponse: The ruler attracts the paper pieces due to induced charge.

learningOutcomesCovered: Apply Coulomb's law to calculate force between point charges
Explain the fundamental properties of electric charge
Draw and interpret electric field lines
Understand the concept of electric flux
Connect theoretical concepts to real-world phenomena
